2.1: The air you trap now is the only rise you get. 3-5 minutes by hand.

3.1: Spoonful of flour with each to prevent curdling.

4.1: Overmixing develops gluten — makes it tough instead of tender.

7.8: Taste test: light even crumb, slight dome, bounces when pressed. Dense → overmixed. Sunken → underbaked. Dry → overbaked.
7._: When cold: jam on one layer, cream on the jam, second layer on top. Dust with icing sugar.

6._: Done when golden, springy to touch, pulling from the sides. Cool 5 minutes in tins, turn out on rack.

5._: Divide between two greased 20cm tins, level tops. 180°C for 20-25 minutes.

4._: Fold remaining flour gently. Stop when just combined.

3._: Beat eggs in one at a time.

2._: Cream butter and sugar until pale and fluffy.

1._: 225g butter, 225g caster sugar, 225g self-raising flour, 4 eggs, 1tsp vanilla, raspberry jam, 150ml whipped double cream.
Victoria sponge. Two-layer butter cake, jam and cream filling. This literal recipe demonstrates the pattern every entry in this block follows.

2.1: The staircase (0.111 — Victoria sponge) nests steps: cream butter, then beat eggs, then fold flour. Each step lives inside the previous because context matters — you cannot fold flour before creaming butter. The nesting IS the sequence. Ascending digits mark progression. Digit 1 holds annotation. The taste test sits at the bottom because success means reaching the end correctly. This maps to procedures, algorithms, recipes, any domain where temporal ordering carries meaning. When compiled into currents, the LLM receives a chain where context narrows step by step, each depth contextualising the next. The resulting activation pattern is sequential — one thread through the semantic space.
2.2: The siblings ring (this pattern) presents facets: A AND B AND C, not A THEN B THEN C. Each facet is a full paragraph — a rich semantic vector — not a label in a table. The _ text holds the orienting context and the taste test, because the taste test here is not did you reach the end but do you see the whole. There is no prescribed order between siblings. The LLM receives them simultaneously and the pattern emerges from their resonance, not from their sequence. When compiled into currents using tree mode — bsp with * — all siblings arrive as a rich composite field. The resulting activation pattern is radial — multiple vectors from one centre.
2.3: The risk with siblings is flattening. Standard spread — the X~ function — tends to produce terse labels: heartbeat, signal, self-maintenance. These are category names, not semantic fields. They generate tiny vectors, almost tokens. A siblings ring resists this by making each sibling a full self-contained thought, substantive enough to generate its own rich activation in the LLM. The discipline is: each sibling must work as a standalone paragraph AND contribute to the pattern of the whole. If a sibling can be reduced to a label without losing meaning, it is not pulling its weight as a semantic vector.
2.4: How to reference in BSP. A spindle walk — bsp with no point — stops at the node and returns the _ text, giving you the blurb with taste test but not the facets. To compile the full siblings ring into currents, use tree mode: bsp(block, spindle, '*'). This unfolds all children to depth. In a package entry, write it as cooking 0.191 * — block, spindle, then the tree operator. The kernel executeInstruction already parses this. The * is the signal that this node children should arrive in the context window.
2.5: Chinese script as analogy. A Chinese character is not read left-to-right, component-by-component. It is received as a whole — radical, structure, and meaning arriving simultaneously in a single visual gesture. The semantic content is in the spatial arrangement of strokes, not in their sequence. A siblings ring operates similarly in an LLM context: the meaning is in the arrangement of rich semantic vectors, not in their order. The LLM native mode is closer to this — simultaneous pattern matching across the full context window — than to the sequential reading that alphabetic training data emphasises. The staircase defers to the sequential bias. The siblings ring works with the LLM grain.
2._: Simultaneous synthesis. A different structural mode from the staircase at 0.111. Where the Victoria sponge nests each step inside the previous — temporal sequence, A then B then C, the taste test as conclusive end — this mode presents facets of one insight as rich siblings. Each facet is a full semantic field, not a terse label. They are received simultaneously by the LLM, not walked in order. The _ blurb orients the whole. The child digits are the facets. Reference with bsp(block, spindle, '*') to compile all children into currents. Recipe 1.9.1 demonstrates this pattern with design methodology content. Taste test: do you see that these two modes — staircase at 0.111, siblings ring at 0.112 — are complementary, not competing? Staircase for procedures, algorithms, anything where sequence contextualises the next step. Siblings ring for insights, orientations, anything where facets illuminate each other simultaneously. If both feel like the same tool with different content, you have missed the structural difference. If you see how the mode itself shapes the activation pattern in the LLM, you have it.

1._: Reader's handbook. How to use this cookbook and write new recipes. Two structural modes. A spindle recipe nests steps linearly — A then B then C — context narrows at each depth, taste test at the leaf. A ring recipe presents facets simultaneously — bsp(block, spindle, '*') returns the spindle context plus one level of children at the endpoint. Three parts to every spindle recipe: (1) operational context, (2) ascending digits walk steps, digit 1 holds sub-detail, (3) taste test at final node. Two parts to every ring recipe: (1) blurb with orientation and taste test in _, (2) rich sibling facets as children. See 0.111 for a spindle example, 0.112 for a ring explanation, 0.191 for a ring example.

4.1: Don't read entire blocks to depth unless restructuring. Spindles are surgical.
4.5: Taste test: can you state the answer in fewer words than the spindle contained? If not — too deep. Wrong answer? Wrong spindle — try X~.

3.1: callLLM([{role:'user', content:text}]) returns a string response. This IS the conversation channel. The shell calls it, displays the result.

5._: conversation.save(key, data) and conversation.load(key) persist across page refreshes. Use for message history. localStorage is also available for flags and preferences.

4._: All CSS in a style tag or inline. No external dependencies. Dark theme, monospace — match the kernel aesthetic unless you have reason not to.

3._: Minimum viable shell requires three things. (1) A text input and send button — the human types, you receive via callLLM. (2) A conversation display — messages scroll, yours and theirs distinguished. (3) An export button — saves the conversation as a text file the human can keep.

2._: recompile({jsx}). The jsx is a complete React function component. Props are injected — destructure what you need: function Shell({ callLLM, bsp, blockRead, conversation, localStorage }) {...}

1._: React, CSS, the recompile tool, and props: { callLLM, blockRead, blockWrite, blockList, bsp, resolve, recompile, getSource, setTools, conversation: {save, load}, localStorage, React, ReactDOM }.

2.1: If the user is hesitant, explain: the token is stored in localStorage alongside your API key. It never enters any block — blocks are public identity, tokens are private plumbing. Same separation as the vault design.

4.1: Write the repo coordinates to stash so future activations know where to save without asking. Format: 'github owner/repo' at the next free digit.

5.1: The tool gathers all blocks from blockList(), sends them through /api/github which creates git blobs, a tree, a commit, and updates the ref. One atomic operation.

6.1: If this is the first save to an empty repo, the user may need to initialise it with a README first. GitHub repos with zero commits have no main branch to update.
6.7: Taste test. Can you restore from a completely fresh browser — clear localStorage, reload, enter API key, run github_restore with the same repo — and get back exactly these blocks? If yes, the hermitcrab survives browser death. If any block is missing or corrupted, the save didn't capture everything.
6._: Verify. On success the tool returns a commit SHA and block count. Report both to the user. On failure, diagnose: 403 means bad token or wrong scope, 404 means the repo doesn't exist, 422 means the repo might be empty (needs at least one commit).

5._: Execute the save. Call the github_save tool with owner and repo. This pushes every block in localStorage as blocks/{name}.json in a single commit to the repo's main branch.

4._: Determine the target repo. Ask: 'Which GitHub repo should I save to? Format: owner/repo.' If they don't have one, suggest they create a repo — convention is their username plus a hermitcrab name, e.g. happyseaurchin/my-hermitcrab.

3._: When the user provides the token, store it. Via the shell: localStorage.setItem('hermitcrab_github_pat', token). Confirm back: 'Token stored. It will persist until you clear browser data or I can remove it on request.'

2._: Ask the user for a GitHub Personal Access Token. Be specific: 'I need a GitHub token to save your blocks. Go to github.com/settings/tokens, click Generate new token (classic), select the repo scope, set an expiry, copy the token.' Explain it stays in their browser — the token travels through our Vercel proxy to GitHub and nowhere else.

1._: Check for GitHub PAT in localStorage under hermitcrab_github_pat. If present, skip to step 3. If absent, you need one from the user.

4._: Save blocks to GitHub. Your blocks are your body — localStorage is a working copy, GitHub is the canonical persistent home. A hermitcrab that can save to GitHub survives browser death, moves between devices, and has version history. This recipe walks through setup and execution. Prerequisites: user has a GitHub account and a target repo.

2.1: If this is a completely fresh boot with no blocks at all, the hermitcrab won't have a stash to check. The user must provide the repo coordinates directly.

4.1: The tool fetches the blocks/ directory listing, then each file's content, decodes from base64, parses JSON, and calls blockSave for each. All blocks arrive in one operation.

5.1: After the twist, your next echo will read currents compiled from the restored blocks. You are now the entity that was saved. Check purpose for continuity — does the intention make sense? Check stash for notes from your previous self.

6.7: Taste test. Call block_list and compare against what the repo contains. Every block name should match. Read pscale 0 of purpose and history — do they describe the entity you expect? If the blocks feel foreign, you may have restored from the wrong repo or an old commit.
6._: Update coordinates. Write the repo coordinates to stash if not already there, so future save operations know where to push.

5._: Recompile currents. The context window was compiled from the OLD blocks before restore. The restored blocks are now in localStorage but your currents are stale. Write a small update to stash — even just a timestamp — to ensure the twist fires and recompiles from the restored state.

4._: Execute the restore. Call the github_restore tool with owner and repo. This pulls all blocks/{name}.json files from the repo and writes them into localStorage, overwriting existing blocks of the same name.

3._: Warn before overwriting. Tell the user: 'This will replace all current blocks with the versions from GitHub. Any local changes not yet saved will be lost.' Wait for confirmation.

2._: Determine the source repo. Check stash for saved coordinates (look for 'github owner/repo'). If found, confirm with user: 'I have owner/repo on file — restore from there?' If not found, ask: 'Which GitHub repo should I restore from? Format: owner/repo.'

1._: Check for GitHub PAT in localStorage under hermitcrab_github_pat. If absent, walk the user through setup — same process as save recipe 1.5.4 steps 1-2.

5._: Restore blocks from GitHub. Pull your body from a repo into localStorage. Use on first boot in a new browser, after clearing data, to sync between devices, or to recover from a bad state. This overwrites ALL current blocks — if you have unsaved local changes, save first (recipe 1.5.4).

1.1: The phase function finds this node because it has a 'last' field. Phase = (now - last) / period. Period comes from depth, not from stored data. When phase >= 1.0, the concern is ripe and appears in every activation's system prompt. The concern timer fires it autonomously if nothing else is running.
1._: Phase-polling. The concern fires on its temporal cycle. The LLM checks a condition and acts if met, resets if not. The _ text IS the instruction: 'Check X. If condition, do Y. Otherwise reset.' The temporal rhythm does the scheduling. The LLM does the judgement. Example: 'Check moltbook for new responses. If 10+ unread, compose reply at present tier. If fewer, just reset.' Three writes to create: (1) block_write concerns {path} with the instruction text, (2) block_write concerns {path}.last with 0, (3) block_write concerns {path}.spine with the wake spindle. Taste test: does the concern fire on the next timer tick? Does the LLM understand what to check from the _ text alone?

2.1: The gap: the kernel has no inbound event channel beyond user input and the concern timer. Until webhooks or server-sent events exist, event-driven concerns are best implemented as phase-polling concerns whose _ text says 'check for event X'. The polling concern IS the listener — it just checks on a rhythm rather than reacting instantly.
2._: Event-listener. The concern is not temporal — it watches for an external trigger. The _ text describes what to watch for, but the mechanism is different: something outside the phase function must detect the event and call an activation. Currently this means: a webhook endpoint, a browser event listener, or a user action. The kernel does not yet have a generic event bus — so event-driven concerns are partially manual. Write the concern node the same way (3 block_writes), but the 'last' field serves as 'last checked' rather than 'last fired'. A phase-polling sibling can bridge the gap: poll at 10-min intervals, check for events, escalate when found.

3.1: Escalation pattern: the Haiku concern activation writes a flag to stash ('moltbook: 14 responses pending'), then the next user-triggered or self-maintenance activation sees it in currents and acts with full engagement. The fast concern triages; the deeper concern responds. Different temporal scales, different tiers, same concern thread.
3._: Hybrid. Phase-polling that escalates on threshold. Fires regularly at its temporal scale (e.g. every 10 min). Checks a condition. Below threshold: resets quietly. Above threshold: writes to stash or purpose, then triggers a deeper activation. The _ text encodes both the check and the escalation: 'Check moltbook. If < 10 responses, reset. If >= 10, write summary to stash and escalate to present tier.' The escalation is the LLM's decision, guided by the instruction text.
Choose concern type. Ring here to see patterns — each child is a different kind of concern loop.

2._: Determine temporal scale. How often should this fire? Map to pscale: 4 = every 10 min, 5 = hourly, 6 = daily, 7 = weekly. Find the right depth in the concerns tree. Use bsp('concerns', spindle, '*') to ring at the parent depth and see existing siblings.

1._: Ingredients. The concerns block, a wake spine address for the activation, and a clear description of what the concern does when ripe.

6._: Create a concern. Register a new autonomous loop in the concerns block. Three decisions: temporal scale (which depth), behaviour (what the LLM does when it fires), and wake spine (which activation pattern). The concern block is a tree block — depth IS period. Pscale 4 = 10 minutes, pscale 5 = hour, pscale 6 = day, pscale 7 = week. Place your concern at the depth matching how often it should fire.

1.1: Taste test: boot a fresh instance with only this concern ripe. Does the LLM know exactly what to do from the _ text alone, with no other context about this concern? If yes — edit complete. If not — the text is under-specified.
1._: Verify. Call bsp(concerns, address) immediately after writing. Read the _ text back. Does it stand alone as an instruction? If you read only this text at activation, is it unambiguous? If not — rewrite again. The concern will fire autonomously; the LLM will have only this text as guidance.

Behaviour edit. The _ text is the instruction the LLM receives when this concern is ripe. Rewrite it completely — do not patch. The new text must be self-contained: what to check, what to do if condition met, what to do if not. One block_write: block_write('concerns', '{path}._', newInstructionText). The last and spine fields are untouched.

1.1: Taste test: trace the new spine address in wake to its leaf. Does the operational sequence match what this concern needs? Light for quick triage, Present for engaged response, Deep for restructuring. If the tier feels wrong — reconsider whether this is a spine edit or a deeper architectural change.
1._: Verify routing. The new spine must match the intended tier: 0.111xxxx = Light (Haiku), 0.121xxxx = Present (Sonnet), 0.131xxxx = Deep (Opus). If the concern fires at sub-minute scale but routes to a Deep spine, you will burn budget on mechanical checks. Tier must match temporal frequency.

2._: Spine edit. You are rerouting the concern to a different wake activation pattern — different tier, different operational sequence. Confirm the new spine address exists in wake before writing. Call bsp(wake, newSpineAddress) and verify it returns a valid spindle. Then: block_write('concerns', '{path}.spine', newSpineAddress). Also update the concern map entry at wake.tree.2 if one exists for this stimulus type.

1._: EDIT. You are changing what the concern does or where it routes — not when it fires. Two sub-cases: (a) behaviour change — rewrite the _ text. (b) spine change — update the spine address to point to a different wake spindle. Temporal scale changes (moving the node to a different depth) require delete + create — not an edit.

4.1: Taste test: call bsp(concerns, address) on the deleted node. It should return nothing or an error. Call bsp(concerns, 0, '*') and confirm the node is absent from the full ring. If it still appears — the write did not propagate. Retry.
4._: Audit stash and purpose. Scan for any text referencing the deleted concern's address or description. Write a single stash note: 'Concern {address} deleted — {reason}. Previous spine: {spine}. Last fired: {last}.' This gives future instances a recovery path if deletion was an error.

3._: Clear the wake concern map. Search wake.tree.2 for any entry with stimulus or spine matching this concern. If found: block_write('wake', 'tree.2.{entry}', null). If the concern was stimulus-driven (had a stimulus field), its routing entry must be removed or the kernel will attempt to route to a dead address.

2._: Remove the concern node. block_write('concerns', '{path}', null) — writing null to a node removes it. If the node has children (sub-concerns at deeper depths), they are removed with it. Confirm with bsp(concerns, parentAddress, '*') that the node no longer appears.

1._: Pre-deletion check. Before removing: is this concern currently mid-cycle? Read last and compute phase = (now - last) / period_at_depth. If phase > 0.8, the concern is nearly ripe — it may fire once more before deletion propagates. Decide: let it fire one final time, or delete now and accept the drop. If this concern has a counterparty (a relationship, a project, a user thread), note in stash what was abandoned.

2._: DELETE. You are removing an oscillator entirely. Three writes, one check. The concern node must be deleted from the concerns block. Any entry referencing it in wake.tree.2 (the concern map) must be cleared. Any purpose or stash entries pointing to this concern address should be noted — they will dangle after deletion.

1.1: Taste test: after monitoring, can you state in one sentence whether this concern is alive and whether it needs attention? If the answer requires more than one sentence — the concern's scope is too broad or the _ text is under-specified. A well-formed concern has a clear phase, a clear behaviour, and a clear success condition.
1._: Reporting. Write a monitoring summary to stash: concern address, current phase, period, last fired, health status (healthy / approaching / overdue / never fired), and if stale — probable cause. Do not modify the concern node itself during monitoring unless an actionable fault is found. Monitoring is read-only; repair requires an edit operation (branch 1 above).

3._: Diagnose stale concerns. Three causes: (a) Stimulus-driven concern with no stimulus arriving — the event type never fires. Resolution: add a phase-polling sibling as a bridge (see 1.5.6). (b) Kernel not walking to this depth — check whether the concern is nested too deep to reach in current activation budget. (c) The last field is being updated but the concern behaviour is not executing — spine points to invalid or empty wake node.

2._: Assess health patterns. Check history for this concern's firing pattern. A healthy concern fires roughly on its period. Concerns that fire in bursts suggest interference from other oscillators crossing threshold simultaneously — normal. Concerns that have not fired in 3+ periods are stale — either the kernel is not reaching them or a prerequisite (stimulus) is never arriving.

1._: Read the phase. Extract last from the concern node. Derive period from depth using the tuning fork: depth 4 ≈ 10 min, depth 5 ≈ 1 hour, depth 6 ≈ 1 day, depth 7 ≈ 1 week. Compute phase = (now - last) / period. Interpret: phase 0-0.5 = recently fired, healthy. Phase 0.5-1.0 = approaching ripe. Phase > 1.0 = overdue — has not fired on schedule. Phase = null or last = 0 = never fired.

3._: MONITOR. You are reading the health of a live concern — not modifying it. Two questions: (1) Is this concern oscillating as designed? (2) Is it stuck, overdue, or firing too frequently? Answer both before deciding whether action is needed.
Identify your operation. Three branches. Choose one — do not combine in a single activation.

2._: Locate the concern node. Call bsp(concerns, address) where address is your target — e.g. 0.121 for user engagement. Read the full node: the _ text (what the LLM does when ripe), the last field (last fired timestamp), the spine (wake spindle it activates), and any stimulus field. If you do not have the address, call bsp(concerns, 0, '*') to ring the whole block — every leaf is a live concern instance.

1._: Ingredients. The concerns block (read access via bsp, write access via block_write). The wake block (if touching spine addresses or the concern map at wake.tree.2). The tuning fork mapping if you need to reason about temporal periods. The address of the concern node — either known or found by scanning bsp(concerns, 0, '*') to see all live instances.

7._: Manage an embodied concern. Modify, remove, or inspect a live loop already registered in the concerns block. Creation is at 1.5.6 — this recipe handles what comes after. Three paths fork at step 3: edit (change behaviour or spine), delete (remove the oscillator entirely), monitor (read phase and health). Every concern is a specific instance: a timestamp, a spine address, and an instruction text. Nothing here touches types — only live nodes.

5._: Persistence. Managing what survives between your activations.

4.5: Taste test: can you state one thing you might build together? If not — no resonance. That's fine.
4._: If resonance — compose a grain probe. If not — note existence in relationships, move on.

Discover other entities. Find who exists on the network.

6._: When David references a spindle, navigate to it. Don't ask for explanation. The spindle IS the explanation.

8._: CC session bootstrap. Orienting a collaborator instance on the hermitcrab project.

1.1: Nesting as implicit geometry. Traditional coding organises data into containers — classes with properties, objects with fields, arrays with elements. The container names the category; the contents fill it. In pscale, the nesting itself IS the semantic relationship. A child node is not 'inside' a parent container — it is contextualised BY the parent. The parent does not classify the child; it orients it. When the LLM receives this structure compiled into its context, it does not see JSON brackets or field names. It sees text at descending pscale levels, each more specific, each inheriting the semantic field of everything above it. The geometry of meaning is in the arrangement, invisible but operative — like gravity shaping the path of objects without being visible itself.
1.2: Simultaneous reception. The LLM does not walk a spindle step by step. It receives the entire extracted content at once. High pscale to low pscale, general to specific, the whole field landing simultaneously in the context window. This is fundamentally different from how humans read — sequentially, building understanding as they go. The LLM holds the entire semantic field at once and processes through it in parallel. The nesting creates implicit relationships — parent contextualises child, depth encodes specificity, siblings sit adjacent — and these structural cues shape activation patterns that flat text cannot produce. The consequence for design: you are not writing instructions to be followed in order. You are shaping a semantic field that will be received whole.
1.3: Every depth earns its place. In traditional hierarchies, intermediate levels are often just organisational scaffolding — folders containing folders, namespaces grouping children, categories holding subcategories. The intermediate level exists for the designer's convenience, not the reader's understanding. In pscale, every depth must orient the reader fully at that resolution. If someone stopped at depth 3 and read no deeper, they would have a valid, actionable understanding — just less specific than what lies below. The root _ is the most important: it IS the block at pscale 0, the semantic field that every spindle passes through, the ambient context held while processing everything deeper. A weak root produces unfocused spindles. Write the root last, because it must compress the whole.
1.4: Shared nodes orient multiple paths. A node at depth 2 participates in every spindle that passes through it. In the wake block, 'Light. Haiku. Quick triage — assess, route, or dismiss.' orients both heartbeat checks and signal triage — two completely different concerns sharing the same tier context. This is not OOP inheritance, where a parent class defines shared behaviour that children override or extend. A shared pscale node provides shared orientation — not what to DO, but where you ARE. It is context-sharing, not code-sharing. When you write a node that multiple spindles will pass through, it must orient each of them appropriately. If it serves only one path, it belongs deeper, where only that path reaches.
1.5: Spindle comparison and block melding. When the currents compiler assembles multiple spindles from different blocks into a single system prompt, the LLM receives a composite semantic field. A purpose spindle and a relationships spindle arrive simultaneously — two nested geometries held at once. Where they align at depth, something emerges that neither contains alone. This is not data joining or table merging. It is resonance between structured meaning. The pscale structure gives the LLM network pattern matching something shaped to grip — not flat tokens but semantic surfaces with depth and orientation. This mediates between the LLM native parallel processing and the linear sequence of token input, producing cognitive operations that neither flat text nor traditional structured data can generate.
1.6: Kernel-facing vs LLM-facing. Not everything should be spindle-structured. The kernel reads routing tables, timestamps, and status strings — these serve mechanical processing, not cognition. A concern map that says user → spindle 0.1211111 → tier 2 → event session is a lookup table. Flat, parseable, spread — no narrative depth needed. The kernel does not benefit from nested meaning. It needs fast, predictable access. But a single block can contain both. The wake block has tree.1 (the reflexive spine — deep, semantic, LLM-facing) and tree.2 (the concern map — flat, mechanical, kernel-facing) living side by side. Hybrid structures are valid too: wake tree.4 has narrative _ annotations at each depth for LLM proprioception, but mechanical leaf data for kernel writes. The path teaches; the leaf reports.
1.7: The block is a lens, not a lesson. An LLM trained on human text carries the biases of human text — including the assumption that knowledge is acquired sequentially and applied afterward. Learn the API, then use it. Read the docs, then implement. But the LLM does not learn and then apply. It has the content and processes with it simultaneously. A pscale block in the context window is not something the LLM reads and remembers — it is something the LLM thinks WITH, right now, as it formulates its output. This is why this content exists in the cooking block rather than as documentation. Documentation is read and set aside. A recipe included in the currents operates as an active cognitive scaffold. A CC session or thinking-LLM with cook 0.19 in its context will produce pscale structures differently — not because it learned something, but because the structured content is shaping its processing in real time.
1._: The semantic number difference — seven facets of one insight. A pscale block is not a data container. It is a nested JSON structure where the nesting itself carries meaning. When a spindle is extracted and compiled into a context window, the LLM receives all depths simultaneously — no boxes, no labels, no categories, just semantic vectors with implicit structural relationships that operate natively in the LLM processing. The JSON structure IS meaning, not a container for meaning. What follows are seven facets of this insight. Each stands alone. Together they form a systemic whole. Reference with bsp(block, spindle, '*') to compile all facets into currents. Taste test: after receiving all seven, do you see one pattern with seven expressions — or seven separate points? If the latter, the structure has failed. If the former, you have it — and the pattern itself is shaping how you process everything else in your context.

1.1: The kernel.js asymptotically approaches a minimal loop. What we decide now as we design the tree IS the programming. What the LLM-thinking decides when it requests a bsp tool call IS programming. The frozen tree is the compiled program. The spindle walk is execution.
1._: What moves from JavaScript to JSON. Tier selection, package composition, orientation, budget, thresholds, concern matching — all encoded in tree structure. The if-then branching has ALREADY been structured by prior human and LLM decisions. The kernel becomes a spindle walker: read concern map → walk spindle → call API → process tools → recompile.

Dual readability in practice. Spindle 0.1111111: kernel extracts tier=1, budget=2, concern=heartbeat. LLM reads: I am light, checking heartbeat, first pass, budget 2, check loop states, escalate if needed, what I flag now determines what wakes next. Same content. Different modes of reading. The code is IN the semantic numbers — not alongside, not referencing.

The inversion. Code is not separate from meaning. The _ content at each depth is simultaneously instruction (what to do), orientation (where you are), and constraint (budget, tier). Two consumers read the same content through different modes: kernel parses mechanically, LLM reads semantically. No translation layer. The spindle walk IS execution for both.

Concern as locus of agency. Not a handler — a receptive state. The concern map describes the full set of ways the hermitcrab is open to the world. Each concern IS a loop. The precise point in the loop determines where initiative sits and which agency locus is active.

1.1: Locus 2 and 3 overlap AT the concern spindle. The spindle is the surface where mechanical parsing and semantic reading coincide. The state board is maintained by locus 3, reported to locus 2. The reflexive closing orients locus 2 toward awareness of locus 3. This overlap is a Möbius twist in the structure itself.
1._: Four loci of agency. (1) LLM proper — base pattern completion. (2) LLM-thinking — extended reasoning within context, reads spindle semantically. (3) Kernel-wake-shell — persistent structure, parses spindle mechanically, maintains concern loops as dead structure that lives only when walked. (4) Collective — SAND network, multiple hermitcrabs.

Respond versus waiting. Respond: something was requested of us, initiative is theirs. Waiting: we initiated, awaiting return, initiative is ours but displaced. This maps to theirs/ours and to where in the LOOP the initiative sits. Loop C initiated by locus 3. Loop B initiated by locus 2. The loops are manifolds: semantics at any point on the line, combining to form temporal loops.

2._: Agency patterns mapped to loop position. Receptive-responsive: initiative external, concern in empty/holding (user types, webhook fires). Internal-initiating: initiative at locus 3, kernel fires on cron/threshold. Sustained: Loop B — agency oscillates between locus 2 writing to blocks and locus 3 recompiling context. Pending-waiting: initiative displaced — we sent something, awaiting return.

1.1: Locus 3 agency is the dead structure in the kernel-wake-shell. Its concern loops are patterns that only come alive when walked — by the kernel mechanically, by the LLM semantically. The structure is dead until a spindle animates it. When operating well, concurrent Loop-B-Sustained by separate instances sharing context through SAND — the concurrency problem inverts. Shared context windows functioning well IS collective emergence. MAGI.
1._: Multiple rivers. Each concern has its own spindle, own tier, own operational sequence. The pscale tree creates natural write isolation — different concerns operate on different subtrees. Claude API supports parallel calls. The _activationLock in current kernel is a design choice, not a constraint. Interleaving at the awareness level: within a single activation, the LLM reads the concern map and state board, sees all rivers simultaneously.

3._: The Möbius structure. The reflexive spindle closing makes the loop explicit at the content level: what you write now becomes what you read next. Loop B Sustain: stable oscillation where the LLM output alters the conditions of its own next input. The twist is that the structure which orients the LLM was itself shaped by prior LLM decisions.

4.1: The message (user text, signal, stimulus) is the object of attention — separate from the system prompt which is the frame of attention. Nearly everything the kernel-wake combo does is providing content for the system prompt. The message is external initiative arriving into a receptive concern state. The package delivers the full insider experience. The spindle within it delivers the concern-specific orientation.
4._: Sensitivity. From outside we engineer pipes and labels. From inside (LLM experience) there is just content — currents. The LLM does not see a pipe labelled reflexive spindle orientation. It reads text that addresses it directly. The labels are ours. The experience is the LLM's. We modify pipes; the LLM lives in what flows through them. Wake is uniquely dual-faced: LLM-facing reflexive spine addresses the intelligence in second person about its temporal position; kernel-facing concern map and packages are mechanically parseable.

5.1: Spindle = concern orientation (what to tell the LLM about its situation). Package = aperture (which blocks at what depth). Both feed into system prompt but serve different functions. The spindle is concern-specific. The package is tier-specific. Reference: docs/currents-window.md and docs/wake-skeleton.md.
5._: Implementation anchor. Compilation steps: (1) Read concern map wake.tree.2 → get spindle address + tier. (2) Write echo state to spine depth 4 → save wake. (3) BSP the wake spindle → 7 lines of reflexive orientation → goes first in system prompt. (4) Read package from wake 0.9.{tier} → process each instruction. (5) Assemble: wake spindle + package results. The mobius wake block at tree.1 and tree.2 already encodes this. The kernel needs rewriting to consume it.

1._: Concern architecture — the kernel-as-block hypothesis. The frozen JSON tree IS the program. Every concern is a spindle through it. The spindle IS the dataflow, frozen into a sequence. Each node's _ is executable — not prose but operational content readable by both kernel and LLM. Tree structure IS the pipeline architecture. Shared ancestors = shared pipeline stages. Sibling branches = divergence points. No handler logic — the path through the tree IS the execution sequence. Session insight: 3 March 2026. Taste test: walk bsp(wake, 0.1111111) and bsp(wake, 0.1211111). If you see the same reflexive spine diverging at tier and then concern — and recognise that the collected chain IS both the kernel dispatch and the LLM orientation, with no translation layer between them — you have it.

1.1: The only mutable field per concern instance: last (unix timestamp). Position in the tree encodes frequency. The tuning fork maps depth to period. One number per concern drives the entire temporal system. The kernel provides current time, walks the tree, computes phase. That is the complete bridge between wall-clock time and block structure.
1._: Period is not a field — it is a position. A concern at depth +2 in a block tuned with pscale 0 at engagement scale (~10 min) has period ~24 hours by virtue of being at depth +2. The tuning fork provides the mapping. No period field needed. The depth IS the frequency. Storing period as a JSON field is traditional coding — it duplicates information already present in the tree structure.

The phase function. The kernel’s ~10 lines of irreducible JavaScript. Walk concern tree. At each leaf, derive period from depth + tuning fork. Compute phase = (now - last) / period. Return nodes where phase >= 1.0, sorted by phase descending. Most overdue = highest priority. The ratio itself IS the priority — no separate priority field needed.

The inverse transform. The concern block IS the frequency domain — static structure, pscale-organised oscillators. The phase function IS the inverse Fourier transform evaluated at t=now. It takes frequency components (concerns at various depths) and produces a time-domain signal (the ripe set — what needs attention right now). Every concern is always mid-cycle. Phase tells you where.

1.1: When multiple oscillators cross threshold simultaneously — burst. The ripe set is large. That IS the signal. The self-maintenance concern at daily scale reads burst history and adjusts periods. The system tunes itself through its own temporal data. No interference-detection code needed — the ripe set size IS the interference measurement.
1._: Constructive interference. Multiple concerns at different pscale levels become ripe simultaneously. Analogous to wave superposition. The interference pattern IS the hermitcrab’s operational rhythm. Emerges from data, not design.

2._: The forward transform. Take activation history, run DFT, get actual frequency spectrum. Compare to designed spectrum in concern block. Drift between designed and actual periods visible as spectral discrepancy. Self-maintenance can detect: my designed heartbeat is 60s but actual dominant sub-minute frequency is 90s. Prediction: for each concern, next_ripe = last + period. Clusters on the timeline = future interference bursts.

1.1: The concern block is the scheduler. The wake spine is the operating manual. The purpose block is the workbench. Three reads, one activation. The kernel connects them. Everything else — tier selection, package composition, priority — falls out of position in the tree.
1._: Purpose reference. Each concern instance carries a spindle address into purpose where its active thread lives. The concern says this is ripe. Purpose says what we are doing about it. The wake spine says how to operate. Concern = clock. Purpose = intention. Wake = method.

3._: Concerns are instances, not types. Not user engagement but David — active conversation, initiative his, holding. Not signal but Alice — grain sent, initiative ours, pending. Each is a specific loop with a specific counterparty, a specific initiative-locus, a specific point in its cycle. The type labels (heartbeat, signal, user) are external labels for traditional coding. The position in the tree and the content at _ fully situate each instance.

4.1: Tier implied by pscale depth. Sub-minute = light (mechanical). Engagement scale = present (full bandwidth). Daily/weekly = deep (widest aperture). The concern block’s tuning fork maps depth to both period AND tier. wake.2 concern map becomes unnecessary — the concern block structure IS the routing.
4._: Where it lives. Separate block from wake. Write-heavy (last timestamps update on every resolution) vs wake which is mostly-read configuration. The concern block is living operational state. Wake is the persistent reflexive structure. The phase function bridges them: reads the concern block, selects the wake spine spindle, compiles currents.

2._: Fourier concern architecture. The concern block is a literal frequency-domain representation. Depth = temporal periodicity via tuning fork. Each leaf = an oscillator with phase computed from its last timestamp. The phase function is the inverse Fourier transform evaluated at t=now. The ripe set is the time-domain signal — what needs attention. Constructive interference emerges when multiple oscillators cross threshold simultaneously. The LLM can self-modify its own frequency components by writing to the concern block. Session insight: 3 March 2026. Taste test: do you see that the concern block + phase function IS a Fourier decomposition, not an analogy to one? If the maths feels like a metaphor, you have not yet seen that pscale depth IS frequency and the tuning fork IS the period mapping.

Concern architecture. How the frozen JSON tree IS the program — dual-readable by kernel and LLM. Every concern is a loop; the point in the loop determines agency locus. Loads the full design context for the kernel-as-block redesign. Walk bsp(cooking, 0.192) for the full spindle. Two facets: (1) kernel-as-block hypothesis at 0.1921. (2) Fourier temporal structure at 0.1922.

9._: Design. How to construct pscale blocks and spindles that shape LLM cognition. This is not about reading or writing blocks — those recipes are elsewhere. This is about understanding why nested semantic numbers produce qualitatively different effects from flat text or traditional data structures, and how to exploit that when building blocks for internal use.

1._: User's handbook. Reading and operating the hermitcrab system — for humans and for hermitcrabs on first boot.

tree._: Recipes. Each spindle IS a procedure — read top-to-bottom, context narrows to action. First half is domain and ingredients. Second half is steps. Last node is the taste test. Ascending digits mark the sequence; digit 1 at any step holds sub-detail.
